Numerical Linear Algebra

Least Squares Approximation

Least Squares Approximation

Real-world data is messy. If you measure five points that are supposed to lie on a line, they almost never do — noise, rounding, and imperfect models mean a handful of data points rarely fit any tidy formula exactly. Least squares is the standard way to find the “best” approximate answer when no exact answer exists.

This chapter builds the idea in three passes. First, we motivate it geometrically as a projection problem. Second, we turn that geometry into an algebraic recipe (the normal equations) and apply it to concrete curve-fitting examples. Third — and this is where the previous chapter’s machinery pays off — we revisit the problem using the QR factorization, which solves it in a numerically safer way than the normal equations alone.

1. The Overdetermined System

Overdetermined system

A linear system Ax=bAx = bAx=b is overdetermined when there are more equations than unknowns: A∈Rm×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n with m>nm > nm>n. Geometrically, we are asking nnn unknowns to simultaneously satisfy more constraints than degrees of freedom available to satisfy them.

For an overdetermined system, an exact solution Ax=bAx = bAx=b typically does not exist — bbb simply doesn’t lie in the right place for that to happen. Instead of giving up, we ask a more modest question: what is the vector x^\hat{x}x^ that gets Ax^A\hat{x}Ax^ as close as possible to bbb?

Geometric perspective

The columns of AAA span a subspace of Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm called the column space, Col⁡(A)\operatorname{Col}(A)Col(A). The equation Ax=bAx = bAx=b has an exact solution only when bbb happens to land inside this subspace. When it doesn’t — which is the typical case — the best we can do is find the point in Col⁡(A)\operatorname{Col}(A)Col(A) that is closest to bbb.

That closest point has a name: it’s the orthogonal projection of bbb onto Col⁡(A)\operatorname{Col}(A)Col(A), an idea that connects directly back to the projector matrices from the previous chapter. Just as Pu=uuTP_u = uu^TPu​=uuT projected a vector onto a single direction uuu, here we’re projecting onto the entire span of AAA‘s columns.

Why orthogonality is the right criterion

If Ax^A\hat{x}Ax^ is the closest point in Col⁡(A)\operatorname{Col}(A)Col(A) to bbb, then the leftover error — the residual r=b−Ax^r = b - A\hat{x}r=b−Ax^ — must be perpendicular to every vector in Col⁡(A)\operatorname{Col}(A)Col(A). If it weren’t, you could tilt Ax^A\hat{x}Ax^ slightly toward bbb along the column space and shrink the error further, contradicting that Ax^A\hat{x}Ax^ was already the closest point.

Writing “perpendicular to every vector in Col⁡(A)\operatorname{Col}(A)Col(A)” algebraically: for every x∈Rnx \in \mathbb{R}^nx∈Rn, (Ax)T(b−Ax^)=0⟹xTAT(b−Ax^)=0.(Ax)^T(b - A\hat{x}) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x^TA^T(b-A\hat{x}) = 0.(Ax)T(b−Ax^)=0⟹xTAT(b−Ax^)=0. Since this holds for all xxx, the vector AT(b−Ax^)A^T(b-A\hat{x})AT(b−Ax^) itself must be zero — giving us the central result of this chapter.

Normal Equations (Projection Condition)

Let A∈Rm×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n and b∈Rmb\in\mathbb{R}^mb∈Rm. A vector x^\hat{x}x^ minimizes ∥Ax−b∥2\|Ax-b\|_2∥Ax−b∥2​ if and only if the residual r:=b−Ax^r := b - A\hat{x}r:=b−Ax^ is orthogonal to Col⁡(A)\operatorname{Col}(A)Col(A): AT(b−Ax^)=0⟺ATA x^=ATb.A^T(b - A\hat{x}) = 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad A^TA\,\hat{x} = A^Tb.AT(b−Ax^)=0⟺ATAx^=ATb. If AAA has full column rank, the solution is unique: x^=(ATA)−1ATb.\hat{x} = (A^TA)^{-1}A^Tb.x^=(ATA)−1ATb.

Proof sketch

The condition r⊥Col⁡(A)r \perp \operatorname{Col}(A)r⊥Col(A) means (Ax)Tr=0(Ax)^Tr = 0(Ax)Tr=0 for every x∈Rnx\in\mathbb{R}^nx∈Rn, i.e. xTAT(b−Ax^)=0x^TA^T(b-A\hat{x}) = 0xTAT(b−Ax^)=0 for all xxx. This holds for every xxx exactly when AT(b−Ax^)=0A^T(b-A\hat{x}) = 0AT(b−Ax^)=0, which rearranges to the normal equation ATAx^=ATbA^TA\hat{x} = A^TbATAx^=ATb.

2. Application: Fitting Data

The normal equations aren’t just an abstract projection formula — they’re the engine behind curve fitting. The idea: choose a model, express “how well the model fits” as a distance-minimization problem, and let the normal equations find the optimal parameters.

Residual, SSE, RMSE

For data {(xi,yi)}i=1N\{(x_i,y_i)\}_{i=1}^N{(xi​,yi​)}i=1N​ and model predictions y^i\hat{y}_iy^​i​:

  • Residual vector: r=b−Aθ^r = b - A\hat{\theta}r=b−Aθ^
  • Sum of Squared Errors (SSE): ∥r∥22=∑iri2\|r\|_2^2 = \sum_i r_i^2∥r∥22​=∑i​ri2​
  • Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE): SSEN\sqrt{\dfrac{\mathrm{SSE}}{N}}NSSE​​

Fitting a model amounts to choosing parameters θ\thetaθ to minimize SSE(θ)=∑i(yi−y^i)2=∥Aθ−b∥22\mathrm{SSE}(\theta) = \sum_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \|A\theta - b\|_2^2SSE(θ)=∑i​(yi​−y^​i​)2=∥Aθ−b∥22​ — precisely the least-squares objective from Section 1, with AAA built out of the data.

Algorithm: Linear Least Squares via Normal Equations
  1. Gather data {(xi,yi)}i=1N\{(x_i,y_i)\}_{i=1}^N{(xi​,yi​)}i=1N​.
  2. Choose a model that is linear in its parameters (e.g. y^=ax+b\hat y = ax+by^​=ax+b, or y^=ax2+bx+c\hat y = ax^2+bx+cy^​=ax2+bx+c — nonlinear in xxx is fine, as long as it’s linear in a,b,c,…a,b,c,\dotsa,b,c,…).
  3. Build the design matrix AAA and right-hand side bbb by substituting the data into the model.
  4. Solve the normal equations ATA θ=ATbA^TA\,\theta = A^TbATAθ=ATb for θ\thetaθ.
  5. Evaluate the fit with SSE / RMSE, and refine the model if needed.

The requirement that the model be linear in its parameters is what keeps this an ordinary least-squares problem — the unknowns a,b,c,…a, b, c,\dotsa,b,c,… appear only as coefficients multiplying known data-derived quantities, so the whole setup can still be written as Aθ=bA\theta = bAθ=b even when the underlying curve (a parabola, say) is not itself a straight line.

3. Worked Examples: Fitting a Line and a Parabola

Throughout, we use the data set (−1,1), (0,0), (1,0), (2,−2)(-1,1),\ (0,0),\ (1,0),\ (2,-2)(−1,1), (0,0), (1,0), (2,−2).

Best-fit line: y^=ax+b\hat y = ax+by^​=ax+b

Substituting each data point into the model gives one row of the system per point:

A=[−11011121],b=[100−2].A = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1\\ 0 & 1\\ 1 & 1\\ 2 & 1\end{bmatrix},\qquad b = \begin{bmatrix}1\\0\\0\\-2\end{bmatrix}.A=​−1012​1111​​,b=​100−2​​.

Forming the normal equations ATA θ=ATbA^TA\,\theta = A^TbATAθ=ATb:

[6224][ab]=[−5−1]⟹a=−0.9, b=0.2.\begin{bmatrix}6 & 2\\ 2 & 4\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}a\\b\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\-1\end{bmatrix} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad a = -0.9,\ b = 0.2.[62​24​][ab​]=[−5−1​]⟹a=−0.9, b=0.2.

Result: y^=−0.9x+0.2\hat y = -0.9x + 0.2y^​=−0.9x+0.2

Best-fit parabola: y^=ax2+bx+c\hat y = ax^2+bx+cy^​=ax2+bx+c

Same data, richer model — now the design matrix has a column for x2x^2x2, xxx, and the constant term:

A=[1−11001111421],b=[100−2].A = \begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 & 1\\ 0 & 0 & 1\\ 1 & 1 & 1\\ 4 & 2 & 1\end{bmatrix},\qquad b = \begin{bmatrix}1\\0\\0\\-2\end{bmatrix}.A=​1014​−1012​1111​​,b=​100−2​​.

Solving the resulting normal equations gives: a=−0.25,b=−0.65,c=0.45⟹y^=−0.25x2−0.65x+0.45.a = -0.25,\quad b = -0.65,\quad c = 0.45 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \hat y = -0.25x^2 - 0.65x + 0.45.a=−0.25,b=−0.65,c=0.45⟹y^​=−0.25x2−0.65x+0.45.

The residuals at x=−1,0,1,2x=-1,0,1,2x=−1,0,1,2 come out to (0.15, −0.45, 0.45, −0.15)(0.15,\ -0.45,\ 0.45,\ -0.15)(0.15, −0.45, 0.45, −0.15), giving SSE=0.45\mathrm{SSE}=0.45SSE=0.45 and RMSE=0.45/4≈0.335\mathrm{RMSE} = \sqrt{0.45/4}\approx 0.335RMSE=0.45/4​≈0.335. As expected, adding a degree of freedom (the quadratic term) lets the model track the data more closely than the line did.

Linear-in-parameters models, more generally

Nothing above required xxx itself to appear linearly — only the parameters had to. The same recipe works for any model of the form y^(t)=c1ϕ1(t)+c2ϕ2(t)+⋯+cpϕp(t)\hat y(t) = c_1\phi_1(t) + c_2\phi_2(t) + \cdots + c_p\phi_p(t)y^​(t)=c1​ϕ1​(t)+c2​ϕ2​(t)+⋯+cp​ϕp​(t) for arbitrary (even nonlinear) basis functions ϕj\phi_jϕj​ — trigonometric terms, powers, exponentials of a fixed rate, and so on. Build AAA with entries Aij=ϕj(ti)A_{ij} = \phi_j(t_i)Aij​=ϕj​(ti​), and the normal equations apply exactly as before.

4. Data Linearization for Genuinely Non-Linear Models

Sometimes the parameters themselves enter non-linearly, and no amount of clever basis functions will turn the model into an Aθ=bA\theta=bAθ=b system directly. If the data is strictly positive, logarithms often come to the rescue by converting a multiplicative, nonlinear relationship into an additive, linear one.

Exponential model

y=c1ec2ty = c_1 e^{c_2 t}y=c1​ec2​t. Taking logs of both sides: ln⁡y=ln⁡c1+c2t.\ln y = \ln c_1 + c_2 t.lny=lnc1​+c2​t. Substituting Y=ln⁡yY = \ln yY=lny and k=ln⁡c1k = \ln c_1k=lnc1​ gives the linear model Y=c2t+kY = c_2 t + kY=c2​t+k — an ordinary line-fitting problem in the transformed variable YYY. Recover c1=ekc_1 = e^kc1​=ek afterward.

Power-law model

y=c1tc2y = c_1 t^{c_2}y=c1​tc2​. Taking logs: ln⁡y=ln⁡c1+c2ln⁡t.\ln y = \ln c_1 + c_2 \ln t.lny=lnc1​+c2​lnt. Substituting Y=ln⁡yY=\ln yY=lny, X=ln⁡tX = \ln tX=lnt, k=ln⁡c1k=\ln c_1k=lnc1​ gives Y=c2X+kY = c_2 X + kY=c2​X+k — again a line-fitting problem, now in ln⁡t\ln tlnt vs. ln⁡y\ln ylny space.

Caveat: this changes the objective

Fitting a line to (ln⁡t,ln⁡y)(\ln t, \ln y)(lnt,lny) minimizes squared error in log space, not in the original yyy-space. This is a different (and not necessarily equivalent) notion of “best fit” from minimizing ∑(yi−y^i)2\sum (y_i - \hat y_i)^2∑(yi​−y^​i​)2 directly. Always keep domain restrictions in mind too — y>0y>0y>0 and t>0t>0t>0 are required for the logarithm to be defined.

5. Better Alternatives: QR and SVD

The normal-equations approach is conceptually clean, but it has a serious practical flaw, and this is where the previous chapter’s QR factorization becomes directly useful.

Remark on numerical stability

Forming ATAA^TAATA squares the matrix’s condition number: κ2(ATA)=κ2(A)2.\kappa_2(A^TA) = \kappa_2(A)^2.κ2​(ATA)=κ2​(A)2. A moderately ill-conditioned AAA can become severely ill-conditioned once squared, amplifying rounding error and making x^=(ATA)−1ATb\hat x = (A^TA)^{-1}A^Tbx^=(ATA)−1ATb unreliable in floating-point arithmetic — even though the formula is mathematically exact. This is precisely the failure mode that orthogonal matrices are designed to avoid (recall: orthogonal transformations preserve length and don’t amplify error).

Solving least squares with QR — without ever forming ATAA^TAATA

This is where the Householder QR factorization from the previous chapter pays off directly. Substitute A=QRA = QRA=QR into the least-squares objective: min⁡x∥Ax−b∥2=min⁡x∥QRx−b∥2.\min_x \|Ax-b\|_2 = \min_x \|QRx-b\|_2.minx​∥Ax−b∥2​=minx​∥QRx−b∥2​.

Because QQQ is orthogonal, multiplying by QTQ^TQT inside the norm doesn’t change its value (this is the same length-preservation property that made reflectors and rotators safe to chain together in the previous chapter): ∥QRx−b∥2=∥QT(QRx−b)∥2=∥Rx−QTb∥2.\|QRx-b\|_2 = \|Q^T(QRx-b)\|_2 = \|Rx - Q^Tb\|_2.∥QRx−b∥2​=∥QT(QRx−b)∥2​=∥Rx−QTb∥2​.

Let d=QTbd = Q^Tbd=QTb. Since RRR is upper triangular, it has a nonzero block only in its top nnn rows (an n×nn\times nn×n block R1R_1R1​), with zeros below. Splitting ddd to match:

∥[R10]x−[d1d2]∥2\left\|\begin{bmatrix}R_1\\0\end{bmatrix}x - \begin{bmatrix}d_1\\d_2\end{bmatrix}\right\|_2​[R1​0​]x−[d1​d2​​]​2​

The bottom block contributes ∥−d2∥2\|{-d_2}\|_2∥−d2​∥2​ regardless of xxx — there’s no way to reduce it, since it’s multiplied by a block of zeros. So the norm is minimized by making the top block vanish exactly:

Least squares via QR

Solve R1x=d1R_1 x = d_1R1​x=d1​ by back-substitution (fast, since R1R_1R1​ is triangular). The minimum achievable residual norm is ∥d2∥2\|d_2\|_2∥d2​∥2​.

Notice what didn’t happen here: at no point did we form ATAA^TAATA, so we never squared the condition number. This is why numerical libraries prefer QR (or the related SVD-based approach below) over the normal equations whenever conditioning is a concern.

The SVD alternative

An even more robust (but more expensive) option is the singular value decomposition A=UΣVTA = U\Sigma V^TA=UΣVT, giving x^=A+b=VΣ+UTb,\hat x = A^+b = V\Sigma^+U^Tb,x^=A+b=VΣ+UTb, where A+A^+A+ is the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse. SVD handles rank-deficient AAA gracefully and returns the minimum-norm solution when the least-squares solution isn’t unique — the most numerically robust option of the three, at the cost of more computation than QR.

Summary

ApproachFormulaNumerical behavior
Normal equationsx^=(ATA)−1ATb\hat x = (A^TA)^{-1}A^Tbx^=(ATA)−1ATbSimple, but κ(ATA)=κ(A)2\kappa(A^TA) = \kappa(A)^2κ(ATA)=κ(A)2 — can be unstable
QR factorizationSolve R1x=d1R_1x = d_1R1​x=d1​, d=QTbd = Q^Tbd=QTbAvoids forming ATAA^TAATA; standard choice in practice
SVDx^=VΣ+UTb\hat x = V\Sigma^+U^Tbx^=VΣ+UTbMost robust; handles rank deficiency; most expensive

The throughline: least squares begins as a geometric projection problem (project bbb onto Col⁡(A)\operatorname{Col}(A)Col(A)), becomes an algebraic recipe (the normal equations) for fitting curves to data, and finally reconnects to the orthogonal-matrix toolkit of the previous chapter — QR factorization lets us solve the very same projection problem while sidestepping the numerical fragility of ATAA^TAATA.
